q: Hyperglycemia can be caused by severe stress such as a myocardial infarction or trauma.
e: Sever stress (transient effect) such as trauma, myocardial infarction or cerebrovascular accidents can cause hyperglycemia.

q: Transient hyperglycemia is never caused by cerebrovascular accidents.
e: Hyperglycemia may be due to sever stress (transient effect) such as trauma, myocardial infarction or cerebrovascular accidents.

q: Diabetes mellitus is characterized by an absolute or relative lack of insulin.
e: DM: Syndrome characterized by hyperglycemia due to an absolute or relative lack of insulin, and/or insulin resistance.

q: According to the laboratory findings defined by WHO, a fasting venous plasma glucose concentration of 7.0 mmol/l or more indicates diabetes.
e: It has been defined by WHO, on the basis of laboratory findings, as a fasting venous plasma glucose concentration or (F.B.S) of 7.0 mmol/l (80-100 mg/dl) or more.

q: An oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) is strictly forbidden to establish a diabetes diagnosis.
e: Sometime an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) may be required to establish the diagnosis.

q: Diabetes is considered a major cause of lower limb amputation.
e: Diabetes is a major cause of : ... Lower limb amputation.

q: Type 1 diabetes is also known as Non-insulin dependent diabetes mellitus.
e: Type 1 diabetes: Called insulin dependent diabetes mellitus.

q: Type 1 diabetes accounts for approximately 10% of diabetic patients.
e: ~10% of diabetic patients.

q: The peak incidence of Type 1 diabetes occurs between 9 and 14 years of age.
e: Most common in young (peak incidence 9-14 years of age).

q: Type 1 diabetes is characterized by an autoimmune destruction of beta cells.
e: Absolute lack of insulin production and secretion by the beta cells of the pancreas due to autoimmune destruction of beta cells.

q: Type 2 diabetes is caused by an absolute lack of insulin production from the start.
e: Type 2 diabetes features a decline in insulin action due to the resistance of tissue cells to the action of insulin, and serum levels may initially be normal or high.

q: In Type 2 diabetes, tissue cells exhibit resistance to the action of insulin.
e: Decline in insulin action due to the resistance of tissue cells to the action of insulin.

q: Type 2 diabetes accounts for only about 10% of all diabetic patients.
e: Majority of diabetic patients (90%).

q: Type 2 diabetes usually occurs in individuals over 40 years of age.
e: Can occur at any age (Usually>40yrs).

q: Serum insulin levels in Type 2 diabetes may initially be normal or even high.
e: Serum insulin levels may be normal or even high.

q: Type 2 diabetes is rarely associated with obesity.
e: Commonly associated with obesity.

q: End-stage Type 2 diabetes can eventually lead to beta cell failure and insulin deficiency.
e: in the end β cell failure and insulin deficiency.

q: High levels of Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) are considered a risk factor for Type 2 diabetes.
e: Risk factors for type 2 diabetes: ... density lipoprotein (LDL)↑.

q: Lack of exercise and high blood pressure are both risk factors for Type 2 diabetes.
e: Risk factors for type 2 diabetes: ... Lack of exercise ... High blood pressure.

q: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) involves a combination of relatively inadequate insulin secretion and responsiveness.
e: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) resembles type 2 diabetes in several respects, involving a combination of relatively inadequate insulin secretion and responsiveness.

q: Gestational diabetes occurs in about 20% to 50% of all pregnancies.
e: It occurs in about 2%-5% of all pregnancies.

q: Gestational diabetes may improve or completely disappear after delivery.
e: may improve or disappear after delivery.

q: About 20%-50% of women affected by gestational diabetes develop Type 2 diabetes later in life.
e: About 20%-50% of affected women develop type 2 diabetes later in life.

q: Untreated gestational diabetes poses no health risks to the fetus.
e: Even though it may be transient, untreated gestational diabetes can damage the health of the fetus or mother.

q: Macrosomia is a risk to the baby if the mother has untreated gestational diabetes.
e: Risks to the baby include: Macrosomia (high birth weight).

q: Skeletal muscle malformations in the baby can be a complication of gestational diabetes.
e: Risks to the baby include: ... Skeletal muscle malformations.

q: One of the classic clinical features of diabetes is unexplained weight gain.
e: Clinical features of diabetes: ... 2. Weight loss.

q: Polyuria is a common clinical feature of diabetes.
e: Clinical features of diabetes: ... 5. Polyuria (increased urine volume).

q: Normal fasting venous plasma glucose is defined as being greater than 6.1 mmol/L.
e: Normal fasting venous plasma glucose: <6.1mmol/L OR between (80-100) mg/dl.

q: A fasting venous plasma glucose of ≥ 126 mg/dl is a diagnostic criterion for diabetes.
e: Diabetes symptoms: 1. Fasting venous plasma glucose ≥7.0mmol/L OR (≥ 126) mg/dl.

q: A random plasma glucose of ≥ 11.1 mmol/L is diagnostic for diabetes.
e: Diabetes symptoms: ... 2. Random plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L OR (≥200)mg/dl.

q: Glucagon stimulates the breakdown of glycogen in the liver.
e: Figure (2-3) shows Glucagon points to 'Stimulates glycogen breakdown' in the Liver.

q: Insulin directly promotes the release of glucagon from the pancreas.
e: High blood sugar promotes insulin release, while low blood sugar promotes glucagon release.

q: Insulin stimulates glucose uptake from the blood into tissue cells.
e: Figure (2-3) shows Insulin points to 'Stimulates glucose uptake from blood' into Tissue cells.

q: Low blood sugar directly promotes the release of insulin from the pancreas.
e: Low blood sugar Promotes glucagon release. High blood sugar Promotes insulin release.

q: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is one of the causes of coma in a diabetic patient.
e: Causes of coma in the diabetic patient: ... Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).

q: HONK stands for Hypo-osmolar non-ketonic hyperglycemia.
e: Hyperosmolar non-ketonic hyperglycemia (HONK).

q: Hypoglycemia is the least common cause of coma in diabetic patients.
e: Hypo glycaemia: Most common cause of coma in diabetics.

q: Plasma glucose levels below 2.5 mmol/L indicate hypoglycemia.
e: Plasma [glucose] < 2.5 mmol/L (< 70 mg/dl).

q: Hypoglycemia can be triggered by a missed meal or excessive exercise.
e: Due to too high a dose of insulin ... Missed meal or excessive exercise.

q: Signs of hypoglycemia include sweating, anxiety, tremor, and confusion.
e: Signs -sweating, anxiety, tremor, confusion, dizziness, hunger.

q: Treating hypoglycemia may involve receiving 10-20 g of carbohydrate.
e: Raising the blood sugar to normal through: 1.Receiving 10-20 g of carbohydrate can give intravenous dextrose.

q: An intramuscular injection of 1 to 2 mg of insulin is used to treat severe hypoglycemia.
e: 2. 1 to 2 mg of glucagon in an intramuscular injection.

q: Irreversible neurological damage can occur if severe hypoglycemia is not treated promptly.
e: This can be treated in the emergency situation by giving glucose intravenously to avoid irreversible neurological damage.

q: Patients on insulin are advised to monitor their blood glucose closely, particularly before driving.
e: It is important for patients on insulin to monitor their own blood glucose closely, particularly if they wish to drive.

q: The presented case study 2 describes a patient in a coma due to hyperosmolar non-ketonic hyperglycemia (HONK).
e: The patient was shown to have Type 1 diabetes mellitus and had presented in diabetic ketoacidosis.

q: According to the case study discussion, diabetic ketoacidosis can present with hypernatremia and hyperkalemia.
e: had presented in diabetic ketoacidosis, with hyperglycemia, hypernatremia, hyperkalemia and a metabolic acidosis.

q: A metabolic alkalosis is a common finding in a patient presenting with diabetic ketoacidosis.
e: had presented in diabetic ketoacidosis, with hyperglycemia, hypernatremia, hyperkalemia and a metabolic acidosis.

q: Diabetes can lead to an increased risk of severe infections.
e: Clinical features of diabetes: ... 4. Infections.

q: Insulin stimulates glycogen formation in the liver.
e: Figure (2-3) shows Insulin pointing to 'Stimulates glycogen formation' in the liver.
